metadata.description: Legal glossary for plain language simplification

Acquittal.simple: When a court decides you are not guilty of the charges
Acquittal.legal: A judgment rendered by a court that a defendant is not guilty of the charges brought against them
Acquittal.example: After the trial concluded, the judge announced an acquittal for Mr. Singh

Advocate.simple: A lawyer who represents people in court
Advocate.legal: A member of the legal profession qualified to appear in court and represent clients in legal matters
Advocate.example: We hired an advocate to fight our case in the district court

Bail.simple: Money paid to be released from jail while waiting for trial
Bail.legal: A security deposit paid to the court to secure the temporary release of an accused person pending trial
Bail.example: The judge set bail at ₹10,000, and we paid it to release him from custody

Bailable.simple: A crime where the accused can be released by paying money (bail) until trial
Bailable.legal: An offense in which the accused has the right to be released on bail as a matter of right
Bailable.example: Theft is a bailable offense, so he was granted bail of ₹25,000

Case.simple: A legal matter that is taken to court
Case.legal: A legal dispute or criminal matter formally brought before a court of law for adjudication
Case.example: The case against the accused will be heard next month in court

Cognizable.simple: A crime serious enough that police can arrest without permission
Cognizable.legal: An offense in which police have the power to search, seize, and arrest without warrant
Cognizable.example: Murder is a cognizable offense, so police arrested the suspect immediately

Complaint.simple: A formal written statement of a wrong or injury to the police or court
Complaint.legal: A formal allegation or accusation made to the police or court regarding a crime or civil matter
Complaint.example: I filed a complaint with the police about the theft

Conviction.simple: When a court finds someone guilty after trial
Conviction.legal: A judgment of a court that a defendant is guilty of a crime as charged
Conviction.example: The conviction resulted in a 5-year prison sentence

Cross-Examination.simple: When the other side's lawyer questions your witness to check if they're telling the truth
Cross-Examination.legal: The questioning of a witness by the opposing counsel during trial to test credibility and contradiction
Cross-Examination.example: During cross-examination, the defense lawyer challenged the witness's testimony

Discharge.simple: When a court decides there is not enough evidence to continue the case
Discharge.legal: An order by the court to dismiss the charges against an accused when no prima facie case is established
Discharge.example: The judge ordered discharge of the accused in the mistaken identity case

Evidence.simple: Facts or information that help prove what happened
Evidence.legal: Any matter, whether oral, documentary, or material, admissible in court to prove a fact in issue
Evidence.example: The CCTV footage is credible evidence of who committed the theft

FIR.simple: A written report made to police when a crime is committed
FIR.legal: First Information Report - a detailed account of a crime filed with the police to initiate investigation
FIR.example: I went to the police station and filed an FIR for the robbery

Grievance.simple: A formal complaint about something that is wrong or unfair
Grievance.legal: A formal statement expressing dissatisfaction regarding a breach of law or unfair treatment
Grievance.example: I filed a grievance against the police for harassing my family

Guilty.simple: When someone is found to have done the crime they were accused of
Guilty.legal: Having been found by a court to have committed the offense charged
Guilty.example: The jury decided that the defendant was guilty beyond reasonable doubt

Hearing.simple: When the judge listens to both sides argue their case in court
Hearing.legal: A formal court session where arguments and evidence are presented before a judge
Hearing.example: The hearing is scheduled for next Tuesday at 2 PM in the district court

Injunction.simple: A court order stopping someone from doing something or making them do it
Injunction.legal: A court order prohibiting a person from taking a specific action or requiring them to take action
Injunction.example: The court issued an injunction stopping the factory from polluting the river

Jurisdiction.simple: The authority of a court to hear and decide a case
Jurisdiction.legal: The power and authority of a court to hear a case and make decisions within its territorial limits
Jurisdiction.example: The district court has jurisdiction over this case because the crime occurred in this district

Liability.simple: Legal responsibility for something, such as paying damages
Liability.legal: Legal obligation or responsibility to pay damages or fulfill a duty imposed by law
Liability.example: The company was held liable for the injury caused by their faulty product

Mediation.simple: When a neutral third person helps two sides reach agreement without court
Mediation.legal: A dispute resolution process where parties attempt resolution with assistance of a neutral mediator
Mediation.example: The mediation helped settle the dispute between the two neighbors

Negligence.simple: Failure to take care, resulting in harm to someone
Negligence.legal: The failure to exercise reasonable care resulting in damage or injury to another party
Negligence.example: The doctor's negligence during surgery caused permanent damage to the patient

Not Guilty.simple: When a court decides you did not commit the crime
Not Guilty.legal: A verdict where the accused is found to have not committed the offense(s) charged
Not Guilty.example: The jury returned a verdict of not guilty, and the defendant was acquitted

Petition.simple: A formal written request to the court for help or action
Petition.legal: A formal document submitted to court requesting relief or action under law
Petition.example: We filed a petition in the high court challenging the government order

Plea.simple: A statement in court saying whether the accused admits or denies the crime
Plea.legal: A defendant's statement before the court admitting or denying the charges
Plea.example: The accused entered a guilty plea at the district court

Precedent.simple: An earlier court decision that influences how similar cases are decided
Precedent.legal: A legal principle or rule established by a previous court decision and followed in similar cases
Precedent.example: The higher court's precedent set the standard for all lower courts in this matter

Proof.simple: Facts or evidence that show something is true
Proof.legal: Evidence or information sufficient to establish a fact to the required standard of proof
Proof.example: The prosecution provided proof of the defendant's guilt beyond reasonable doubt

Sentence.simple: The punishment given by a judge after someone is found guilty
Sentence.legal: The punishment imposed by a court after conviction of an offense
Sentence.example: The judge sentenced him to 7 years in prison for the crime

Settlement.simple: When both sides agree to end the case without going to trial
Settlement.legal: An agreement between parties to resolve a dispute without continuing judicial proceedings
Settlement.example: The parties reached a settlement of ₹ 5 lakhs to end the dispute

Statute.simple: A law passed by the government
Statute.legal: A formal written enactment of the legislative body prescribing rules and regulations
Statute.example: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) is a statute that defines crimes and punishments

Subpoena.simple: A court order making someone appear in court to give evidence
Subpoena.legal: A writ commanding a person to attend court and give testimony or produce evidence
Subpoena.example: The witness received a subpoena requiring her to appear in court

Testimony.simple: What a witness says under oath in court
Testimony.legal: Evidence given by a witness, usually under oath, before a court
Testimony.example: The victim's testimony was crucial in convicting the defendant

Trial.simple: When a court hears all the evidence and decides if someone is guilty or not guilty
Trial.legal: A formal proceeding before a court in which evidence is presented to determine guilt or innocence
Trial.example: The trial lasted three weeks before the jury gave their verdict

Verdict.simple: The decision a court makes about whether someone is guilty or not guilty
Verdict.legal: A finding or decision by a court or jury regarding guilt or innocence based on evidence
Verdict.example: The jury returned a verdict of guilty after deliberating for two hours

Warrant.simple: A court order giving police permission to arrest someone or search property
Warrant.legal: A written order issued by a judge authorizing law enforcement to arrest or search
Warrant.example: The judge issued an arrest warrant for the suspect

Witness.simple: A person who saw or knows something about what happened
Witness.legal: A person who has personal knowledge of facts and gives evidence in court or deposition
Witness.example: The shop owner was the main witness to the robbery

Writ.simple: A written court order commanding someone to do or not do something
Writ.legal: A formal written order issued by a court commanding a specific action or remedy
Writ.example: The high court issued a writ of habeas corpus on behalf of the detained person
